coredata.dc:title: Enhancement of the luminescent thermal stability and water resistance of K2SiF6:Mn4+,Na+ by double coating of GQDs and K2SiF6
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Yan
Wang, Tianman
Deng, Daishu
Zhong, Xue
Li, Yuelan
Wang, Lin
Liao, Sen
Huang, Yingheng
Long, Jinqiao
coredata.dc:description: Nowadays, it is still a big challenge to synthesize Mn4+-doped fluoride red phosphors with high water resistance, luminescent intensity and thermal stability simultaneously. Therefore, KSF:M,N@GQDs@KSF (K2SiF6:0.06Mn,0.10Na@GQDs (12 mg/mol)@K2SiF6 (0.2 wt), KSF: K2SiF6, GQDS: OH-contained graphene quantum dots) with high luminescent intensity, high luminescent thermal stability and water resistance have been synthesized via a combination of H2O2-free room temperature reaction method, hydrothermal coating method and room temperature surface coating process. Experimental results show that enhancement of negative thermal quenching (NTQ) effect and water resistance can be synergistically induced by double coating of GQDs and KSF. After being immersed in deionized water for 360 min, emission intensities of the KSF:M,N@GQDs and KSF:M,N@GQDs@KSF are decreased from 100% to 70.57% and 91.63%, respectively, indicating that water resistance of the latter is obviously improved by coating of KSF. The improved water resistance is attributed to preventing hydrolysis of [MnF6]2- on sample’s surface by insoluble coating shell of KSF. A prototype WLEDs (white light-emitting diodes) emitted warm white light (CCT = 4546 K and Ra = 91.3, at a driving current of 20 mA) has been assembled by coating mixture of KSF:M,N@GQDs@KSF, yellow emitting phosphor (YAG:Ce3+) and epoxy resin on blue light InGaN chips. The good performances of the WLEDs suggest that the KSF:M,N@GQDs@KSF has potential applications in blue-based warm WLEDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mn4+-doped fluoride
Surface coating
Graphene quantum dot
Water resistance
Luminescent thermal stability

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21661006 and No. 21965004 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Zhuang Autonomous Region , China (Grant No. 2019GXNSFDA245022 , No. 2020GXNSFAA159036 ), the Scientific Research Foundation of Guangxi University (Grant No. XDZ140116 ), the innovation Project of Guangxi Graduate Education (Grant No. YCSW2020015 ), the Students Experimental Skills and Innovation Ability Training Fund Project of Guangxi University (No. 202010593186 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns and XPS spectra of samples (i-iv), ((i) KSF:M, (ii) KSF:M,N, (iii) KSF:M,N@GQDs, (iv) KSF:M,N@GQDs@KSF): (a) Full XRD patterns, (b) Local expanded XRD patterns, (c) Full XPS patterns, (d) High-resolution XPS of Mn 2p.
SEM image and the corresponding EDS spectra of samples (iii-iv) ((iii) KSF:M,N@GQDs, (iv) KSF:M,N@GQDs@KSF)): (a, c) SEM and EDS of the sample (iii), (b, d) SEM and EDS of the sample (iv).
Luminescent properties of samples (i-iv) ((i) KSF:M, (ii) KSF:M,N, (iii) KSF:M,N@GQDs, (iv) KSF:M,N@GQDs @KSF): (a) and (b) PLE and PL spectra, (c) Decay curves, (d) CIE chromaticity diagram.
The luminescent properties of four samples under different conditions, (ia) KSF:xMn4+, (iia) KSF:M,yNa+, (iiia) KSF:M,N@GQDs (z mg/mol), (iva) KSF:M,N@GQDs@KSF (q wt): (a-d) PL spectra.

Moisture resistance test of samples (iii-iv) ((iii) KSF:M,N@GQDs, (iv) KSF:M,N@GQDs@KSF): (a) Photos of phosphors immersed in deionized water under natural light; (b) Relative emission intensity changing with different immersion times.

Temperature-dependent PL performances of samples (iii, iv), (iii) KSF:M,N@GQDs, (iv) KSF:M,N@GQDs @KSF, (a) and (b) PL spectra; (c) Integrated intensity curve; (d) Chromaticity shift, (e) E
a curves, (f) CIE color coordinates, (g) TG of sample (iv).

Performances of prototype WLEDs using the mixing phosphors of KSF:M,N@GQDs@KSF and YAG:Ce3+ based on InGaN chip under a 20 mA drive current: (a) Electroluminescence spectra, (b) CIE chromaticity diagram, (c) A photo of the white emission spectrum of the WLEDs.

head.ce:title: Enhancement of the luminescent thermal stability and water resistance of K2SiF6:Mn4+,Na+ by double coating of GQDs and K2SiF6

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning, Guangxi 530004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning, Guangxi, 530004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Processing for Non-ferrous Metals and Featured Materials, School of Resources, Environment and Materials, Guangxi University, Nanning, Guangxi 530004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Processing for Non-ferrous Metals and Featured Materials, School of Resources, Environment and Materials, Guangxi University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Processing for Non-ferrous Metals and Featured Materials, School of Resources, Environment and Materials, Guangxi University, Nanning, Guangxi, 530004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangxi Colleges and Universities Key Subject of Material Physics and Chemistry, College of Chemistry & Environment Engineering, Baise University, Baise, Guangxi 533000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Colleges and Universities Key Subject of Material Physics and Chemistry, College of Chemistry & Environment Engineering, Baise University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangxi colleges and universities key subject of material physics and chemistry, College of Chemistry & Environment Engineering, Baise University, Baise, Guangxi, 533000, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning, Guangxi 530004, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nowadays, it is still a big challenge to synthesize Mn4+-doped fluoride red phosphors with high water resistance, luminescent intensity and thermal stability simultaneously. Therefore, KSF:M,N@GQDs@KSF (K2SiF6:0.06Mn,0.10Na@GQDs (12 mg/mol)@K2SiF6 (0.2 wt), KSF: K2SiF6, GQDS: OH-contained graphene quantum dots) with high luminescent intensity, high luminescent thermal stability and water resistance have been synthesized via a combination of H2O2-free room temperature reaction method, hydrothermal coating method and room temperature surface coating process. Experimental results show that enhancement of negative thermal quenching (NTQ) effect and water resistance can be synergistically induced by double coating of GQDs and KSF. After being immersed in deionized water for 360 min, emission intensities of the KSF:M,N@GQDs and KSF:M,N@GQDs@KSF are decreased from 100% to 70.57% and 91.63%, respectively, indicating that water resistance of the latter is obviously improved by coating of KSF. The improved water resistance is attributed to preventing hydrolysis of [MnF6]2- on sample’s surface by insoluble coating shell of KSF. A prototype WLEDs (white light-emitting diodes) emitted warm white light (CCT = 4546 K and Ra = 91.3, at a driving current of 20 mA) has been assembled by coating mixture of KSF:M,N@GQDs@KSF, yellow emitting phosphor (YAG:Ce3+) and epoxy resin on blue light InGaN chips. The good performances of the WLEDs suggest that the KSF:M,N@GQDs@KSF has potential applications in blue-based warm WLEDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Solid-state WLEDs occupy a large share of the solid-state lighting market due to their energy-saving, environmentally friendly, and long-lasting advantages [1–4].

Recently, the most concerned design method of WLEDs is the combination of yellow YAG:Ce3+ phosphor and InGaN blue chip [5]. But, the white light obtained by the design cannot be completely close to natural light due to insufficient red emission components. Due to the unique 3d3 external electron configuration of Mn4+, Mn4+-doped fluorides exhibit a broad absorption in blue region and narrow emission lines at ~ 630 nm. So, the Mn4+-doped fluorides are considered as effective candidates of red emission materials for the WLEDs and have been widely reported by many researchers [6–9].

Although Mn4+ doped fluoride phosphors have unique luminescent characteristics, thermal stability, and high photoluminescent quantum yield, Mn4+ of the phosphors are prone to initiate a hydrolysis reaction in a humid environment. The hydrolysis of Mn4+ into hydroxide and manganese oxide may reduce the luminous intensity of phosphor, thereby limiting the practical application of phosphor [10,11]. In a special crystal field environment, the red emission of the Mn4+-doped fluoride phosphors essentially originates from the [MnF6]−2 octahedral luminescence center. Therefore, preventing the hydrolysis of [MnF6]−2 is the key to keep its persistence in the environment.

Subsequently, some researchers have found that surface modification of the phosphors is a common strategy to improve their moisture resistance. For example, Huang et al. used DL-mandelic acid as a surface passivator to treat K2GeF6:Mn4+, and resulted in improvement of its water resistance [12]. Arunkumar et al. used oleic acid as coating agent for coating of K2SiF6:Mn4+, and greatly enhanced its water resistance [13]. Some researchers reported that K2TiF6:Mn4+@K2TiF6 prepared by using K2TiF6 (matrix) as coating agent also had high water resistance [14]. Although there have been many studies on improving the water resistance of phosphors, few studies have been reported for enhancement of the luminescent thermal stability and water resistance of Mn4+-doped fluosilicate via double coating of GQDs and matrix.

Based on our previous work [15–17], here, we synthesized KSF:M,N@GQDs@KSF by double coating with GQDs and KSF. The as-synthesized sample had high luminescent thermal stability, water resistance and luminescent intensity.
The section of experimental and methodology are described in the Supplementary material. Properties of GQDs are also described in Supplementary materials ( Table 1s, and Figs. 1s-5s).

Fig. 1a depicts the XRD patterns of four samples ((i) KSF:M, (ii) KSF:M,N, (iii) KSF:M,N@GQDs, (iv) KSF:M,N@GQDs@KSF), which the standard card of K2SiF6 (PDF#75–0694) is used as a reference. It can be clearly seen from Fig. 1a that all the diffraction peaks of the four samples can be indexed to a standard pattern of cubic K2SiF6, which all belong to the Fmm (225) space group. The index results are listed in Table 1. The results in Fig. 1a show that the four samples have high crystallinity and none of them have any other impurities. Table 1 shows that the order of the lattice volume size of the samples is (i) > (iv) > PDF#75–0694 > (iii) = (ii), which is supported by the results in Fig. 1b. The ionic radii of Mn4+, Si4+, K+ and Na+ (CN = 6) are 0.53, 0.40, 1.38, and 1.18 Å, respectively [18–20]. Therefore, when some Si4+ ions in the lattice position are replaced by Mn4+ ions, the lattice volume will expand, and results in that volume of the sample (i) is larger than that of PDF#75–0694. Similarly, because the radius of Na+ ions is smaller than the radius of K+, the volume of the sample (ii) is smaller than that of the sample (i). After coating with GQDs, since the content of GQDs is extremely small, when compared with the sample (ii), volume of the sample (iii) does not change. In addition, when compared with the sample (iii), volume of the sample (iv) increases slightly, which is caused by the coating of KSF on its surface.

Fig. 1c-d illustrates the XPS spectra of samples (iii-iv) ((iii) KSF:M,N@GQDs, (iv) KSF:M,N@GQDs@KSF). Fig. 1c shows that elements of the samples (iii)-(iv) are Si, K, F, Na, C and Mn, indicating that the samples contain GQDs, which is supported by our previous report [15,16]. The concentration of Mn4+ and Na+ are so low that their peaks are not obvious. It is worth noting that Fig. 1d shows that the Mn signal of the sample (iv) is much lower than that of the sample (iii), indicating that a KSF protective layer is indeed formed on surface of the sample (iv).

Fig. 2a-b are SEM images of samples (iii)-(iv) ((iii) KSF:M,N@GQDs, (iv) KSF:M,N@GQDs@KSF). The two samples are heterogeneous particles with a length of about 2–4 µm. It is noteworthy that the surface of the sample (iv) is covered with many small particles, indicating that its surface is successfully covered by KSF.

The EDS spectra of samples (iii)-(iv) ((iii) KSF:M,N@GQDs, (iv) KSF:M,N@GQDs@KSF) are shown in Fig. 2c-d. The results prove that the sample (iii) contains Si, K, F, Na, C and Mn elements, while the sample (iv) contains K, Si, and F. The disappearance of Mn, Na, and C elements in the sample (iv) also indicates that its surface has been successfully covered by the KSF.

HR-TEM images of the samples (iii-iv) are depicted in Fig. 6s. It can be seen that there are GQDs on the surface of the former (Fig. 6s(a)), and there is shell layer on the surface of the latter (Fig. 6s(b)). Fig. 6s(b) further confirmed that surface of the sample (iv) has been successfully covered by the KSF.

The luminescent characteristics of four samples ((i) KSF:M, (ii) KSF:M,N, (iii) KSF:M,N@GQDs, (iv) KSF:M,N@GQDs@KSF) are shown in Fig. 3. Fig. 3a-b show that luminescent intensity ratios of the samples (i-iv) ((i):(ii):(iii): (iv)) are 1.00:1.21:1.47:1.71, respectively, which the luminescent intensity ratios of (ii):(i), (iii):(ii), (iv):(iii) are 1.21,1.22,1.17. The results indicate that the luminescent intensity can be enhanced by co-doping of Na+, coating of GQDs and KSF in turn. The results are supported and confirmed by reports of some authors [15,16,21,22]. The reports are as follows: (a) Wang et al. improved the luminescent intensity of Ca2GdTaO6:Mn4+ with co-doping of Na+ [21]. (b) Liu et al. [15] and Li et al. [16] enhanced the luminescent intensity of K2SiF6:Mn4+ via coating of GQDs. (c) Chen et al. [22] reported that the PL quantum yield of K2TiF6:Mn4+ could been obviously increased by coating of K2TiF6. Fig. 3a-b also show that all samples have typical excitation and emission bands, and the positions of the main peaks are almost the same. In the PLE spectra (Fig. 3a), the broad peaks at 356 and 467 nm are caused by the spin-allowed 4A2 g → 4T1 g and 4A2 g → 4T2 g transitions of Mn4+ under octahedral symmetry, respectively [22,23]. In PL spectra (Fig. 3b), there are several sharp peaks in the 600–660 nm range, the strongest peak being at 631 nm, and the PL peaks at 598 (as-ν3), 609 (as-ν4), 614 (as-ν6), 631 (s-ν6), 636 (s-ν4), and 648 nm (s-ν3) can be attributed to the vibrational mode of the [MnF6]2- octahedron, which is derived from the spin-forbidden d-d transition of 2Eg→4A2 g of Mn4+ [22–24].

In PL spectra (Fig. 3b), there is no any emission of the GQDs. From Fig. 1s, it can be learn that GQDs emit blue light under 365 nm UV excitation. But the excitation light used in Fig. 3b is about 467 nm blue light, under the excitation of blue light, GQDs do not emit any light ( Fig. 4s(b)).

The fluorescent lifetime, as an important performance index, can reflect changes of fluorescence intensity over time when the sample is de-excited. Fig. 3c describes decay curves of the samples (iii)-(iv). They can be fitted by a linear function with a constant term. Lifetimes of the two samples are 8.53 and 8.56 ms, respectively. It is not difficult to see that the lifetime of the sample (iv) is longer than that of the sample (iii), indicating that there is energy reflection from KSF (donor) to Mn4+ (receptor) [25].

The QY
i values of the samples (i)-(iv) can be calculated using Eq. (1s) [26], and the results are 83.36%, 86.80%, 88.89%, and 96.92%, respectively. The QY
i value of the sample (iii) is higher than that of the samples (i-ii), indicating that the coating of GQDs can enhance the QY
i of the sample. The QY
i value of the sample (iv) is higher than that of the sample (iii), indicating that the coating of KSF can further improve the QY
i of the sample.

Based on the emission spectrum of Fig. 3b, chromatic coordinates of the samples (i)-(iv) are calculated. The calculated results are as follows: (i) (0.6873, 0.3125), (ii) (0.6863 0.3136), (iii) (0.6858, 0.3141), (iv) (0.6800, 0.3198) (Fig. 3d).

According to the results of chromaticity coordinates, Eq. (2s) is used to calculate the color purity of samples (i)-(iv) [27–29], and the results are 99.32%, 99.75%, 99.89%, and 99.95%, respectively. The results prove that coating of GQDs can make the color purity of the sample become perfect, and then the coating of KSF further improves the color purity of the sample.

In order to obtain the final optimal sample, values of four factors, x, y, z and q, need to be investigated and determined. Here, x, y, z, and q are concentration of KMnO4, Na+, GQDs, and KSF, respectively (x = molar ratio of Mn/(Mn+Si), y = molar ratio of Na/Si, z = (mass of GQDs)/(molar number of KSF: M,N), q = mass ratio of KSF/KSF:M,N@GQDs). Luminescent properties of four samples under different conditions are illustrated in Fig. 4. Fig. 4 shows that intensities of emission spectrum are obviously affected by different concentrations of the four factors. Four PL intensity curves (Fig. 4a-d) are all parabolic non-linear curves with their maximum values, which their corresponding values of x, y, z, and q are 6%, 10%, 12, and 0.20, respectively. In addition, the optimal samples corresponding to the highest points of the four curves are KSF:0.06Mn4+ (KSF:M), KSF:0.06Mn4+,0.10Na+ (KSF:M,N), KSF:0.06Mn4+,0.10Na+ @GQDs (12 mg/mol) (KSF:M,N@GQDs) and KSF:0.06Mn4+, 0.10Na+@GQDs (12 mg/mol)@KSF (0.20 wt) ( KSF:M,N@GQDs@KSF), respectively. It can also be seen from Fig. 4b-d that co-doping of Na+, coating of GQDs and KSF can further enhance the emission intensity of the sample, which are also supported by results of other authors’ reports [15,16,21,22]. For example, Wang et al. reported the luminescent intensity of Ca2GdTaO6:Mn4+ was improved by co-doping of Na+, which could be attributed to the charge compensation effect of Na+ ion [21]. Liu et al. [15] and Li et al. [16] reported that the luminescent intensity of K2SiF6:Mn4+ was enhanced by coating of GQDs, which belonged to the coordination between GQDs and Mn4+. Chen et al. [22] reported that the PL quantum yield of K2TiF6:Mn4+ could be obviously increased by coating of K2TiF6, because that the formation of K2TiF6 shell could cut off the path of energy migration to the surface defects.

In order to study the coating effect of KSF on the water resistance of sample, the samples (iii)-(iv) ((iii) KSF:M,N@GQDs, (iv) KSF:M,N@GQDs@KSF) are put in deionized water to compare their water resistances ( Fig. 5a). Under natural light, it can be observed that when the samples are put into the water, color of the sample (iii) changes quickly, while that of the sample (iv) is almost constant. The results show that the protective layer is formed after KSF is coated on the surface of KSF:M,N@GQDs, which significantly improved the moisture resistance of the sample. Fig. 5b depicts emission intensities of samples (iii)-(iv) ((iii) KSF:M,N@GQDs, (iv) KSF:M,N@GQDs@KSF) immersed in deionized water for different times under excitation of 467 nm. The experimental results show that as the immersion time increases, the emission intensities of the two samples gradually decrease. When immersed in deionized water for 360 min, the emission intensities of the two samples are decreased from 100% to 70.57% and 91.63%, respectively, indicating that water resistance of the sample (iv) is better than that of the sample (iii).

Fig. 5b shows that water resistance improvement of the sample (iv) is similar to that of K2TiF6:Mn4+@K2TiF6 reported by Chen et al. [22], which is attributed to preventing hydrolysis of [MnF6]2- on sample’s surface by insoluble coating shell. Result of Fig. 5b shows that the water resistance of sample (iv) can well meet the environmental stability requirements of WLEDs applications.

Since the operating temperature of high-power LED chips can reach up to 150 °C, luminescent thermal stability of red emitting phosphor is also a key parameter, when evaluates its luminescent performances [31,32]. Fig. 6 illustrates temperature- dependent PL performances of samples (iii-iv), ((iii) KSF:M,N@GQDs, (iv) KSF:M,N@GQDs@KSF), Fig. 6a-b show that the PL intensities of the two samples are significantly affected by temperature, while positions of emission peaks are not influenced by temperature. Integrated PL intensities of samples (iii-iv) changed with temperature are demonstrated in Fig. 6c.

Generally speaking, as the ambient temperature increases, the integrated PL intensities of most rare earth activated red phosphors will decrease [33–35]. But, Fig. 6c shows that the integrated PL intensities of the samples (iii-iv) increase with the increasing temperature, and reach their maximum values at 180 °C. The results of Fig. 6c indicate that the luminescence of the two samples has negative thermal quenching (NTQ) effect, which is also observed in reports of some authors [35–38]. Fig. 6c further shows that curve (iv) is much higher than curve (iii), indicating that the effect of the former is stronger than that of the latter. The maximum values of the two curves at 180 °C also show the difference in the strength of the effect between the two samples: the integrated PL intensity of the sample (iv) is 298% that of the initial value at 30 °C, while the intensity of the sample (iii) is only 217% that of the initial value at 30 °C. The results suggest that the samples (iii-iv) all have high luminescent thermal stability, and that of the latter is higher than that of the former.

As we previously reported, both coating of GQDS [15] and K2SiF6 [16] may all induce strong NTQ effect. The results of Fig. 6c show that after coating of GQDs and then coating of K2SiF6, much stronger NTQ effect can be induced synergistically. Although some Mn4+ activated fluoride red phosphors have thermal quenching phenomenon [36,37], their thermal stability is slightly inferior to that of the sample (iv).

The mechanism of the NTQ effect has been discussed in some reports [26,38,39]. It is suggested that with the increasing of temperature, additional energy of electron traps formed in defects of matrix is transferred to Mn4+, and results in the effect [26,38,39]. It is well known that the energy of the excitation light is constant when the sample is tested. Therefore, it is not difficult to speculate that the extra energy in the electron trap is converted from heat energy. So, mechanism of the NTQ can be further considered as the thermo-optic energy conversion one.

In order to explore the influence of temperature on luminescent performance, Eq. (3 s) is used to calculate the chromaticity shift (ΔE) at different temperatures [40,41]. Fig. 6d shows the calculation results of sample (iii)-(iv). It is not difficult to see from Fig. 6d that the curve of sample (iii) is a linear monotonically rising curve, while the curve of sample (iv) is a nonlinear monotonically rising curve. From Fig. 6d, it can be learn that the ΔE of the sample (iii)-(iv) at 175 °C is 28.84 × 10−3 and 12.51 × 10−3, respectively, both smaller than that of the commercial red-emitting phosphor (CSASNE (44 × 10−3 in 175 °C)). The result shows that luminescent thermal stability of sample (iv) is superior compared with that of sample (iii), indicating that sample (iv) can well meet the needs of WLEDs.

The activation energy (E
a ) of luminescent thermal quenching of sample is also an important parameter. The larger the E
a , the harder it is to occur thermal quenching. The Eq. (4s) [42,43] is used to calculate E
a values of the samples (iii-iv) and results are shown in Fig. 6e. According to the slope of the two lines of Fig. 6e, the E
a values of the two samples are 1.02 and 1.05, respectively, indicating that the order of the luminescent thermal stability is (iv) > (iii).

Fig. 6f is the CIE chromaticity coordinates of samples (iii)-(iv) at different temperatures. Fig. 6f shows that the chromaticity coordinates of the samples (iii)-(iv) in the red region are slightly shifted, indicating that them have good color stability. The change of color coordinates may be a slight broadening of the emission peak.

The TG curve of the sample (iv) is shown in Fig. 6g. From the ambient temperature to 1050 °C, the heating rate is 12 °C / min. As shown in Fig. 6g, the sample (iv) begins to decompose around 105 °C. Since only the adsorbed water is lost, the mass loss of the sample (iv) at 200 °C is only 0.58%, indicating that it has high chemical stability. The results show that the sample can be used in WLEDs devices because of its high luminescent and chemical thermal stability.

To confirm the practical application of the sample (iv) (KSF:M,N@GQDs@KSF) in WLEDs, prototype WLEDs are assembled with the mixed phosphors of YAG:Ce3+ and sample (iv) based on InGaN chip. Fig. 7 depicts the performances of the prototype WLEDs at a drive current of 20 mA. Fig. 7a is the electroluminescent spectrum of the WLEDs.

Based on the spectral data of Fig. 7a, the CIE chromaticity diagram can be obtained and shown in Fig. 7b (0.3579,0.3540), showing that the WLEDs emits warm white light accompanied by CCT = 4546 K and Ra = 91.3. Fig. 7c is the luminescent photo of the WLEDs, and the result also shows that the WLEDs emits warm white light. The above results indicate that the sample (iv) has excellent luminescent properties and it is a promising red phosphor that can be applied in WLEDs for indoor lighting.

To sum up, KSF:M,N@GQDs@KSF with high luminescent intensity, high luminescent thermal stability and water resistance have been synthesized via a combination of H2O2-free room temperature reaction method, hydrothermal coating method and room temperature surface coating process. Experimental results show that after coating of GQDs and then coating of KSF, much stronger NTQ effect can be induced synergistically. So, both KSF:M,N@GQDs and KSF:M,N@GQDs@KSF exhibit high luminescent thermal stability. Mechanism of the NTQ can be further considered as the thermo-optic energy conversion one. When immersed in deionized water for 360 min, emission intensities of the KSF:M,N@GQDs and KSF:M,N@GQDs@KSF are decreased from 100% to 70.57% and 91.63%, respectively, indicating that water resistance of the latter is obviously improved by coating of KSF. The improvement of the water resistance is attributed to preventing hydrolysis of [MnF6]2- on sample’s surface by insoluble coating shell of KSF. A prototype WLEDs emitted warm white light (CCT = 4546 K and Ra = 91.3, at a driving current of 20 mA) has been assembled by coating mixture of KSF:M,N@GQDs@KSF, yellow emitting phosphor (YAG:Ce3+) and epoxy resin on blue light InGaN chips. Good performances of the WLEDs suggest that the KSF:M,N@GQDs@KSF has potential applications in blue-based warm WLEDs.

Yan Yu: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Tianman Wang: Investigation. Daishu Deng: Investigation. Xue Zhong: Investigation. Yuelan Li: Investigation. Lin Wang: Investigation. Sen Liao: Conceptualization, Supervision. Yingheng Huang: Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Jinqiao Long: Writing – review & editing, Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21661006 and No. 21965004), the Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Zhuang Autonomous Region, China (Grant No. 2019GXNSFDA245022, No. 2020GXNSFAA159036), the Scientific Research Foundation of Guangxi University (Grant No. XDZ140116), the innovation Project of Guangxi Graduate Education (Grant No. YCSW2020015), the Students Experimental Skills and Innovation Ability Training Fund Project of Guangxi University (No. 202010593186).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2021.162819.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
